content.text: How BM25 Scores Documents

BM25 (Best Match 25) is a probabilistic ranking function that scores documents by how well their tokens match a query. It extends TF-IDF with two improvements: term-frequency saturation (repeated query terms in a document yield diminishing score increases) and document-length normalisation (longer documents are not unfairly penalised for having higher raw term counts). BM25 has no trainable parameters — its behaviour is fully determined by two interpretable constants k₁ and b.
BM25Retriever Setup and Configuration

LangChain's BM25Retriever wraps the rank_bm25 library. Documents are tokenised at construction time; the BM25 index is built in memory. The default tokeniser splits on whitespace and lowercases tokens — adequate for most prose corpora but suboptimal for code, camelCase identifiers, or multilingual text.

from langchain_community.retrievers import BM25Retriever
from langchain_core.documents import Document

# Install: pip install rank_bm25
corpus = [
    Document(page_content="HNSW uses a hierarchical graph for approximate search",
             metadata={"source": "hnsw-paper.pdf", "page": 1}),
    Document(page_content="IVF partitions the vector space into Voronoi cells",
             metadata={"source": "faiss-docs.pdf", "page": 3}),
    Document(page_content="Product Quantisation compresses vectors to save memory",
             metadata={"source": "pq-tutorial.pdf", "page": 1}),
]

# Build index (one-time, at ingest)
bm25 = BM25Retriever.from_documents(corpus)
bm25.k = 3  # top-k results

results = bm25.invoke("hierarchical graph search")
for doc in results:
    print(doc.page_content[:80])

# Tune k₁ and b via the underlying BM25Okapi object
# rank_bm25 exposes: BM25Okapi(corpus, k1=1.5, b=0.75, epsilon=0.25)
# For code corpora: k1=2.0 (exact token repetition matters more)
# For short passages: b=0.5 (reduce length penalty)
Custom Tokenisation for Technical Corpora

The default whitespace tokeniser discards punctuation, which means BM25 cannot distinguish "LangChain" from "langchain" or tokenise "get_embeddings()" as the three tokens "get", "embeddings". For code documentation, API references, or any corpus with structured identifiers, a custom tokeniser that splits on punctuation and case boundaries substantially improves exact-match recall.

import re

def technical_tokenizer(text: str) -> list[str]:
    """Tokenise text for technical corpora (code, API docs, release notes).

    Splits on: whitespace, punctuation, camelCase boundaries, snake_case.
    Lowercases all tokens. Removes empty strings.
    """
    # camelCase → "camel Case"
    text = re.sub(r'([a-z])([A-Z])', r'\1 \2', text)
    # snake_case / kebab-case → tokens
    text = re.sub(r'[_\-]', ' ', text)
    # split on any non-alphanumeric
    tokens = re.split(r'[^\w]', text.lower())
    return [t for t in tokens if len(t) > 1]  # drop single chars

# Examples:
# "getLangChainVersion()" → ["get", "lang", "chain", "version"]
# "error_code_4xx"        → ["error", "code", "4xx"]

bm25_tech = BM25Retriever.from_documents(
    corpus,
    preprocess_func=technical_tokenizer,
)
bm25_tech.k = 5

results = bm25_tech.invoke("getLangChainVersion streaming callbacks")
Sparse Vectors: SPLADE and Learned Sparse Retrieval

BM25 is a heuristic sparse model — its token weights are computed analytically. SPLADE (Sparse Lexical and Expansion model) is a learned sparse retrieval model that produces sparse vectors over the full vocabulary, similar to BM25 but trained end-to-end on retrieval tasks. SPLADE outperforms BM25 on conceptual queries while retaining exact-match strength, at the cost of requiring a neural model inference pass per document. Pinecone and OpenSearch support SPLADE sparse vectors natively.

# SPLADE via Hugging Face (for local experimentation)
from transformers import AutoModelForMaskedLM, AutoTokenizer
import torch

SPLADE_MODEL = "naver/splade-cocondenser-ensembledistil"
tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(SPLADE_MODEL)
model = AutoModelForMaskedLM.from_pretrained(SPLADE_MODEL)
model.eval()

def splade_encode(text: str, max_length: int = 256) -> dict[str, float]:
    """Return a sparse dict of {token: weight} for text."""
    inputs = tokenizer(text, return_tensors="pt",
                       truncation=True, max_length=max_length)
    with torch.no_grad():
        logits = model(**inputs).logits  # (1, seq_len, vocab_size)
    # Max-pool over sequence, ReLU, log(1+x) weighting
    values = torch.log(1 + torch.relu(logits)).max(dim=1).values.squeeze()
    indices = values.nonzero().squeeze().tolist()
    weights = values[indices].tolist()
    if isinstance(indices, int):
        indices, weights = [indices], [weights]
    tokens = tokenizer.convert_ids_to_tokens(indices)
    return {t: float(w) for t, w in zip(tokens, weights) if w > 0.01}

vec = splade_encode("HNSW hierarchical graph approximate search")
# Top tokens: {'hnsw': 2.3, 'hierarchical': 1.9, 'graph': 1.7, 'approximate': 1.4, ...}
print(sorted(vec.items(), key=lambda x: x[1], reverse=True)[:10])
When to Use Pure Sparse vs Hybrid

Pure sparse retrieval (BM25 or SPLADE) is appropriate when the query always uses exact terminology from the corpus — for example, a code-search system where queries are function names and error messages. Hybrid (sparse + dense) is appropriate when queries mix exact terms with conceptual language. Pure dense retrieval is appropriate when the corpus is small and the embedding model has strong domain coverage. Most production RAG systems benefit from hybrid.
Scenario
Recommended approach
Code search / API lookup
BM25 with technical tokeniser, or hybrid (BM25-heavy 0.6/0.4)
General knowledge Q&A
Hybrid equal weights (0.5/0.5), or dense-heavy (0.3/0.7)
Multilingual corpus
Dense-heavy (0.2/0.8); BM25 vocabulary mismatch across languages
Legal / compliance documents
Hybrid (SPLADE sparse preferred; exact term matching matters)
Serialising and Persisting BM25 Indexes

LangChain's BM25Retriever has no built-in persistence — the index lives in memory and must be rebuilt from documents on each startup. For large corpora this rebuild can take seconds to minutes. Pickle serialisation is the simplest workaround; for production deployments, Elasticsearch or OpenSearch provide a persistent, queryable BM25 index with REST API access.

import pickle, pathlib

def save_bm25(retriever: BM25Retriever, path: str) -> None:
    with open(path, "wb") as f:
        pickle.dump(retriever, f)

def load_bm25(path: str) -> BM25Retriever:
    with open(path, "rb") as f:
        return pickle.load(f)

# At startup: load if cached, else build and save
INDEX_PATH = "/data/bm25_index.pkl"

if pathlib.Path(INDEX_PATH).exists():
    bm25 = load_bm25(INDEX_PATH)
    print("BM25 loaded from cache")
else:
    bm25 = BM25Retriever.from_documents(corpus_docs)
    bm25.k = 6
    save_bm25(bm25, INDEX_PATH)
    print(f"BM25 built and cached: {len(corpus_docs)} docs")

# For Elasticsearch-backed BM25 (no rebuild on restart):
# from langchain_elasticsearch import ElasticsearchRetriever
# es_retriever = ElasticsearchRetriever.from_es_params(
#     url="http://localhost:9200",
#     index_name="my-corpus",
#     body_func=lambda q: {"query": {"match": {"text": q}}},
#     content_field="text",
# )
BM25 Parameter Tuning

The two BM25 parameters have clear interpretations. k₁ controls term-frequency saturation: a low k₁ (1.0) means even a single occurrence of a query term contributes nearly the maximum score — good for short passages. A higher k₁ (2.0–3.0) allows more reward for repeated terms — good for code where the same identifier appearing many times is a stronger match signal. b controls document-length normalisation: b=0 disables it entirely, b=1 fully normalises, and b=0.75 is the well-validated default for prose.

from rank_bm25 import BM25Okapi

# Access underlying BM25 object for parameter control
def build_bm25_retriever_custom(
    docs: list,
    k1: float = 1.5,
    b: float = 0.75,
    top_k: int = 6,
    tokenizer=None,
) -> BM25Retriever:
    """Build BM25Retriever with explicit k1 and b parameters."""
    preprocess = tokenizer or (lambda t: t.lower().split())
    tokenised = [preprocess(d.page_content) for d in docs]

    bm25_obj = BM25Okapi(tokenised, k1=k1, b=b)

    # Wrap in a retriever that uses the custom BM25 object
    retriever = BM25Retriever(vectorizer=bm25_obj, docs=docs, k=top_k)
    return retriever

# Code corpus: higher k1, moderate b
code_bm25 = build_bm25_retriever_custom(
    corpus_docs,
    k1=2.0,   # more reward for repeated exact tokens
    b=0.5,    # code files vary in length — reduce length penalty
    tokenizer=technical_tokenizer,
)

# Prose corpus: default parameters
prose_bm25 = build_bm25_retriever_custom(
    corpus_docs,
    k1=1.5,
    b=0.75,
)
Evaluating BM25 Recall on Your Corpus

Before integrating BM25 into a hybrid pipeline, measure its standalone recall on a labelled evaluation set to understand its contribution. If BM25 recall@10 is below 30%, the corpus vocabulary is too dissimilar from query vocabulary for BM25 to add value — the dense retriever alone will perform better. If BM25 recall@10 exceeds 60%, it is a strong standalone retriever and a hybrid may only marginally improve over BM25 alone. The highest hybrid gains typically occur when BM25 and dense each cover different relevant documents.

# Measure BM25 standalone vs dense standalone vs hybrid
eval_set = [
    {"query": "HNSW ef_construction parameter", "expected_ids": ["hnsw-ref-p4"]},
    {"query": "why does approximate search lose recall", "expected_ids": ["ann-tutorial-p2"]},
    # ... 50+ entries
]

def recall_at_k(retriever, eval_set, k=10):
    hits = 0
    for item in eval_set:
        results = retriever.invoke(item["query"])[:k]
        retrieved = {d.metadata.get("doc_id") for d in results}
        expected = set(item["expected_ids"])
        hits += len(retrieved & expected) / max(len(expected), 1)
    return hits / len(eval_set)

bm25_r = recall_at_k(bm25_retriever, eval_set)
dense_r = recall_at_k(dense_retriever, eval_set)
hybrid_r = recall_at_k(ensemble_retriever, eval_set)

print(f"BM25 recall@10:   {bm25_r:.3f}")
print(f"Dense recall@10:  {dense_r:.3f}")
print(f"Hybrid recall@10: {hybrid_r:.3f}")

# Ideal: hybrid > max(bm25, dense)
# If hybrid ≈ dense: BM25 is not adding coverage → check tokeniser
# If hybrid ≈ bm25: dense is not adding coverage → check embedding model domain
Stop Words and Vocabulary Preprocessing

BM25 scores are dominated by IDF: tokens that appear in most documents receive near-zero IDF and contribute little to ranking. This makes explicit stop-word removal less critical than in classic TF-IDF — common tokens self-suppress. However, domain-specific stop words ("the", "a", "is" in English prose, but also "function", "method", "returns" in API documentation) can be removed to sharpen signal on informative tokens. Removing them reduces index size and slightly improves scoring for queries that share a background vocabulary with the corpus.

import string

# English stop words + common API doc terms
API_STOP_WORDS = {
    "the", "a", "an", "is", "are", "was", "were", "be", "been",
    "to", "of", "in", "and", "or", "for", "with", "on", "at",
    # domain-specific: suppress generic API vocabulary
    "function", "method", "returns", "parameter", "value",
    "class", "object", "type", "default", "optional", "example",
}

def filtered_tokenizer(text: str) -> list[str]:
    """Tokenise, lowercase, remove punctuation and stop words."""
    text = text.lower()
    text = text.translate(str.maketrans('', '', string.punctuation))
    return [t for t in text.split() if t not in API_STOP_WORDS and len(t) > 1]

# Usage
bm25_filtered = BM25Retriever.from_documents(
    corpus_docs,
    preprocess_func=filtered_tokenizer,
)
bm25_filtered.k = 6

# Test: "the method returns a value" → tokens: ["returns", "value"]
# Without filtering: ["the", "method", "returns", "a", "value"]
# All high-IDF tokens are preserved; low-IDF tokens removed
Summary

BM25 is a robust, parameter-free sparse retrieval baseline that excels on exact-match queries — proper nouns, version numbers, error codes, and API names. LangChain's BM25Retriever is a zero-dependency in-memory implementation suitable for corpora up to ~100k documents; use Elasticsearch or Pinecone for larger scale. A custom tokeniser aligned to the corpus vocabulary (camelCase splitting, punctuation handling) substantially improves recall on technical corpora.
k₁=1.5 and b=0.75 are reliable defaults; increase k₁ for code corpora where repeated exact tokens carry more signal.
Use a custom preprocess_func when the corpus contains camelCase identifiers, version strings, or mixed-language text.
Pickle BM25Retriever for fast startup; rebuild nightly when the corpus changes to keep the index fresh.
SPLADE (learned sparse) outperforms BM25 on conceptual queries but requires a neural inference pass — use it when BM25 recall is insufficient and hybrid retrieval is already deployed.
